Reverse-engineer the ARCHITECTURE of: **sourcery (51.17, rank 6)**

THE LEADERBOARD ON OUR EVALUATOR (GPT-5.5) — this is the ONLY board that matters to us:
  cellcog-max 55.78 | WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78 | bodhi 54.07 | lunon 53.51 | dalpha-deepresearch 53.10
  sourcery 51.17 | gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98 (== the "reference", so parity = 0.50) | openai-deepresearch 47.84

NOTE: the widely-cited "ADORE 52.65" is on NEITHER board — treat it as unverified. The legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro
board (qianfan 58.03, ZTE 57.27, ...) is a DIFFERENT evaluator and its numbers are NOT comparable to ours.
We already hold 100-task artifacts for: cellcog, tavily, onyx, deepinsight, baidu-qianfan (in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus).

DO BOTH of these:
1. THE PUBLISHED RECORD. Search hard: arXiv, GitHub, company blog, HF model/dataset card, tech report, WeChat/Zhihu
   posts (several of these are Chinese teams — search Chinese-language sources too: 深度研究, 智能体, 报告生成).
   Is there a paper? Code? A tech report? If there is genuinely NOTHING public, SAY SO — do not invent an architecture.
2. THE ARTIFACT. If we have its reports in /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus (or the Acquire phase just fetched them), READ THEM and measure
   them across MANY tasks, not just task 72. Reverse-engineer the generation process from the text: section/subsection
   structure, paragraph length distribution, whether it uses bullets/tables (and whether that varies BY TASK GENRE),
   how it cites, whether it names studies in prose, how it handles conflicting evidence, whether it has a dedicated
   synthesis/tensions section, whether the writing looks single-pass or multi-step, whether there is evidence of a
   revision/critique pass.

THE QUESTION THAT MATTERS MOST: **how does it COMPOSE the report, and how does it produce SYNTHESIS?**
We are an extraction-grounded fact conveyor — we span-verify every sentence, which forbids cross-source inference.
We score insight 0.4238 (weight 0.32, our worst). Find out what these systems do INSTEAD.

Prefer primary sources. Mark anything unverified. We were burned tonight by a confidently-quoted stale number.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

import json,re,statistics as st
rows=[json.loads(l) for l in open('sourcery.jsonl')]
def words(t):
    # count CJK chars + latin words
    cjk=len(re.findall(r'[一-鿿]',t)); lat=len(re.findall(r'[A-Za-z0-9]+',t))
    return cjk+lat
ws=[words(r['article']) for r in rows]
print('len words(cjk+lat) mean',int(st.mean(ws)),'median',int(st.median(ws)),'min',min(ws),'max',max(ws))
# headings
for r in rows[:0]: pass
h2=[len(re.findall(r'^## ',r['article'],re.M)) for r in rows]
h3=[len(re.findall(r'^### ',r['article'],re.M)) for r in rows]
h4=[len(re.findall(r'^#### ',r['article'],re.M)) for r in rows]
print('h2 mean',st.mean(h2),'h3',st.mean(h3),'h4',st.mean(h4))
tab=[r['article'].count('\n|') for r in rows]
print('table pipe lines mean',st.mean(tab), 'articles with tables', sum(1 for t in tab if t>3))
bul=[len(re.findall(r'^\s*[-*] ',r['article'],re.M)) for r in rows]
print('bullets mean',st.mean(bul))
cit=[len(re.findall(r'\[\d+\]',r['article'])) for r in rows]
print('bracket cites mean',st.mean(cit))
mdlink=[len(re.findall(r'\]\(https?://',r['article'])) for r in rows]
print('md links mean',st.mean(mdlink))
# chinese vs english prompts
zh=[r for r in rows if re.search(r'[一-鿿]',r['prompt'])]
print('zh prompts',len(zh))
EOF

content.content: refs mean 11.91 median 12.0 min 2 max 19 ===== Key Questions Answered ===== len 4325 **Does AI substitute for or complement human labor?** The overwhelming consensus is that AI acts predominantly as a complement at the aggregate level, though with critical exceptions for specific occupations [1][2][3][4][6][8]. AI-focused roles are nearly twice as likely to require complementary skills (resilience, agility, analytical thinking) compared to non-AI roles, with complementary effects up to 1.7 times larger than substitution effects in external labor market spillovers [1]. Simulation evidence demonstrates that most occupations retain residual skills enabling adaptation rather than extinction even after substantial task erosion [2]. However, occupations in the top quartile of automation potential saw a 17% decrease in job postings per firm per quarter after generative AI's introduction, while augmentation-prone occupations experienced a 22% increase [4]. The theoretical framework identifies displacement, productivity, and reinstatement effects operating simultaneously, with the net outcome depending on the pace of new task creation relative to automation [6][13]. **How large are AI's macroeconomic productivity estimates?** Estimates vary enormously depending on methodology and assumptions. Acemoglu (2024) estimates baseline TFP gains of only 0.66% over 10 years (or 0.53% when distinguishing easy-to-learn from hard-to-learn tasks), with GDP effects ranging from 0.93% to 1.56% [3]. By contrast, the BIS New-Keynesian DSGE model projects GDP approximately 30% higher after 10 years under AI adoption with 1.5 percentage-point annual productivity growth [11], while Goldman Sachs projects a 7% increase in annual global GDP ($7 trillion) over a decade [8]. Micro-level productivity evidence consistently documents task-level gains of 15% to 55.8% across occupations [8], but translation to macro outcomes remains limited and uncertain, with OECD survey data finding that 83% of AI-adopting SMEs reported no staffing changes [8]. **Which workers and occupations are most affected?** The literature identifies a clear bifurcation. Automation-prone occupations—including Correspondence Clerks (automation score 0.860), Interpreters and ===== Contradictions & Debates ===== len 6853 ### Aggregate Complementarity vs. Occupational Displacement The most significant tension in this literature is between the net-positive framing of Sources 1 and 2 and the distributional severity documented in Sources 4, 5, and 7. Source 1 finds complementary effects 1.7 times larger than substitution effects [1], and Source 2 argues AI sustains occupational roles through emerging complementarity [2]. Yet Source 4 documents a 17% decline in job postings and 38% decline in new skills for automation-prone occupations [4]; Source 7 finds posting declines accelerating to 18% by the third year post-ChatGPT for high-substitution occupations [7]; and Source 5 shows that automation over 1980–2016 produced wage declines of 8.1% for the least-educated workers [5]. These findings are not necessarily contradictory—they may reflect different levels of analysis. The net-positive aggregate effects in Sources 1 and 2 coexist with sharply negative effects for specific occupational groups, a pattern consistent with Source 5's finding that automation and new tasks jointly explain 67–84% of between-group wage structure changes [5]. The resolution is that AI can be a net complement in aggregate while being a severe substitute for particular occupations and demographic groups. ### Scale of Macroeconomic Impact Source 3's conservative estimates (0.53–0.66% TFP over a decade) [3] stand in sharp contrast to the BIS model's projection of GDP approximately 30% higher after 10 years [11] and to Goldman Sachs and McKinsey projections critiqued in Source 3 [3]. The ratio between the most conservative and most optimistic estimates exceeds 10:1 [8], driven primarily by divergent assumptions about task coverage, diffusion speed, and complementary investment rather than conflicting empirical data [8]. Based on the provided webpage content, I found **no explicit technical documentation** about the research pipeline, system architecture, or underlying models.

**Research Methodology Principles:**
- "Each piece starts from a live strategic question and follows its consequences across domains"

- "Coverage before conclusion" — material includes "primary material, current developments, skeptical viewpoints, and historical context"
- "Structured evidence, not loose summarization" — findings are "shaped from extracted notes, claims, figures, and source-level observations"
- Claims remain "tied to evidence instead of drifting into generic synthesis"

## What Is NOT Disclosed:

The webpage contains **zero information** about:
- Pipeline stages or computational architecture

- Outline generation processes
- Section composition templates
- Synthesis or verification algorithms
- Specific LLM models employed
- Benchmark scores (DeepResearch Bench/RACE)
- GitHub repositories
- Operational costs
- Detailed report structure specifications

**Conclusion:** SourceryIntel markets itself as "Autonomous Research Intelligence" but provides no public technical specifications on the actual autonomous research system architecture on this landing page.

# Sourcery: Autonomous Research Intelligence

## Overview
Sourcery is an autonomous research system that generates citation-grounded reports through a 12-step pipeline. The platform synthesizes evidence from hundreds of sources per report with "100% Citation-grounded" claims and "0 Hallucinated claims."

## Architecture & Pipeline

The system operates through a sequential process:

1. **Understand** – Deconstructs questions to identify assumptions and key dimensions
2. **Plan** – Maps sub-questions and evidence requirements
3. **Generate** – Creates multiple focused search queries for balanced discovery
4. **Search** – Crawls the web for documents, datasets, papers, and reports
5. **Clean** – Removes ads, banners, and irrelevant markup locally
6. **Extract** – Captures facts, numbers, dates, and uncertainty signals into structured notes
7. **Expand** – Recursively follows related URLs and cited sources
8. **Cross-check** – Surfaces contradictions and conflicting interpretations
9. **Remember** – Persists research data for auditability and reuse
10. **Synthesize** – Builds draft insights across evidence chunks
11. **Verify** – Ensures citations map deterministically to real sources
12. **Deliver** – Outputs fully referenced Markdown reports

## Key Differentiators

- **Cost**: Under $0.10 per report (including residential proxy costs)
- **No limits**: Unrestricted by subscription plans or rate caps
- **Depth**: Reportedly outperforms ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude's research features
- **Contradiction preservation**: Conflicting claims are explicitly retained rather than obscured

## Report Structure

Reports feature:
- Inline citations traceable to source material
- 25-35 minute read lengths
- 40-70+ sources per analysis
- Markdown format with preserved contradictions

## Current Reports

Eight published deep dives covering topics including GameStop's eBay bid, AI cybersecurity threats, data center financial risks, energy security, financial infrastructure, and compute asset financialization.

## Availability

**Platform**: Node.js CLI tool  
**Repository**: github.com/freakynit/sourcery (currently private)  
**Status**: Source code publication "very soon"  
**Builder**: Nitin Bansal

# Sourcery Methodology & Technical Architecture

## Pipeline Stages

Sourcery executes research through these sequential steps: "Builds a structured research plan → generates targeted multi-angle search queries → crawls and cleans real web pages (including anti-bot protected ones via residential proxies) → extracts structured claims → recursively follows citations and related sources → detects contradictions across sources, and → synthesizes everything into a fully citation-linked Markdown report"

## Report Composition & Evidence Handling

The system grounds every assertion in traceable sources. As stated: "Every claim in the generated report is linked, and traceable, to the exact source." Notably, "Citations aren't decorative - they're deterministically rebuilt from the actual source list."

## Contradiction Management

Rather than obscuring disagreements between sources, the platform actively surfaces them: "Sourcery doesn't smooth over conflicts; contradictions between sources are surfaced explicitly and carried into the final report."

## Technical Infrastructure

Key components include local HTML extraction (no third-party scraping services), residential proxy integration for bypassing anti-bot protections, local PDF parsing capabilities, and SQLite-based persistence storing "all state (runs, sources, claims, contradictions, memory)."

## Model & Cost Details

The system operates as "LLM-agnostic" and functions "with any OpenAI-compatible inference endpoint," supporting resumable runs with cross-run source memory. Operating costs are extremely low—under $0.10 per execution.

input.composition_method:
SCHEMA-DRIVEN, SECTION-SCOPED COMPOSITION AGAINST A CLAIM STORE. Not a free outline.

1) FIXED GLOBAL TEMPLATE, genre-invariant. 86/100 reports have the IDENTICAL H2 sequence; 100/100 share the core set: TOC > Executive Summary > Key Questions Answered > Core Findings > Contradictions & Debates > Deep Analysis > Implications > Future Outlook > Unknowns & Open Questions > Evidence Map > References. Future Outlook is ALWAYS Optimistic / Base Case / Pessimistic (98/100). It does NOT adapt structure to task genre -- Chinese-language reports (50/100) keep ENGLISH H2 headings while writing Chinese bodies. Same template on academic lit reviews, market sizing, and M&A analysis.

2) SECTION WORD BUDGET (measured): Core Findings 4,777w (40% of report, 7.8 subsections, 81% contain tables) >> Deep Analysis 1,627w > Contradictions 981w > Key Questions 808w > Evidence Map 632w (100% a table) > Executive Summary 595w (pure prose, no bullets/tables) > Future Outlook 586w > Implications 656w > Unknowns 521w.

3) EVIDENCE OF MULTI-PASS / SECTION-SCOPED GENERATION, not one global pass: citation FORMAT differs by section. Corpus-wide, "Unknowns & Open Questions" uses bare [4][7][11] (907 bare vs 57 anchored) while every other section uses HTML anchors (Core Findings 14,904 anchored vs 340 bare). The linkification/verify pass systematically misses one section -> sections are generated and stitched separately, then a deterministic citation-rebuild pass runs over them. Also 64/100 reports LEAK the internal source labels into prose ("Source 4 documents a 17% decline...", 1,470 occurrences) -- proof the composer sees a numbered SOURCE CARD POOL, not raw text.

4) HOW SYNTHESIS IS PRODUCED -- the actual answer:
 (a) The outline is THEMATIC, not per-source. Core Findings subsections draw a mean of 4.94 distinct sources each; 74% cite >=3 sources; only 15% are single-source. So each writing unit is a THEME with a bundle of claims from many sources.
 (b) Insight is emitted as an explicit RELATION between claims, and the relation itself is cited to both sides. 40% of all cited sentences cite >=2 distinct sources. Example (task 72): "The ratio between the most conservative and most optimistic estimates exceeds 10:1, driven by divergent ASSUMPTIONS about task coverage and diffusion speed rather than conflicting empirical data." That is a computed cross-source claim, still fully anchored.
 (c) THREE DEDICATED SYNTHESIS SECTIONS that are renderings of pipeline stages, not creative writing: "Contradictions & Debates" (99/100; 6.6 named tensions each; mean 3.78 distinct sources per tension; each tension states thesis vs antithesis with cites, then RESOLVES it -- e.g. "These findings are not necessarily contradictory -- they reflect different levels of analysis... AI can be a net complement in aggregate while being a severe substitute for particular occupations"); "Evidence Map" (99/100, always a table: Theme x Strongest Evidence / Moderate Evidence / Weak-or-Absent Evidence, with cites in each cell -- a rendering of evidence STRENGTH and GAPS); "Unknowns & Open Questions" (99/100, ~10 numbered gaps, each grounded in what sources FAIL to cover: "Source 4 explicitly lacks data on actual wages").
 (d) Uncited connective prose IS allowed: sentence-level citation coverage is only 65.7% (n=17,160 sentences). ~1/3 of sentences are interpretation, framing, and transition with no span behind them. It does not span-verify every sentence.
 (e) Hedging/contrast markers: 24.8 per report; 68 percent-figures per report. Dense, quantitative, and explicitly uncertain.

5) NO evidence of a global revision/critique loop over the finished draft (heading inconsistencies like "Contradictions & Debates" vs "Contradictions and Debates" and the missed linkification in one section survive to output). The "critique" is upstream, in the Cross-check stage over claims -- not downstream over prose.

input.performance:
On our GPT-5.5 board: overall 51.17 | comprehensiveness 50.53 | insight 52.22 | instruction_following 51.06 | readability 49.68.

Shape: INSIGHT IS ITS BEST DIMENSION and its only clearly above-reference one (+2.24 over the 49.98 gemini reference); readability is its WORST and is BELOW the reference (49.68). This is a system that buys insight at the cost of readability -- the fixed 11-section scaffold, the 4-column Evidence Map table, English headings on Chinese reports, and 372 inline anchor-cites per report all hurt readability. It is also the SHORTEST of the top 6 (11.9k words vs cellcog-max 22.3k, lunon 15.6k) and the most source-frugal (11.9 refs), yet beats gemini/openai on insight. Insight-per-source and insight-per-word are its differentiator, not scale. Its comprehensiveness (50.53) is the weakest of the top 6 -- consistent with mining 12 sources very deeply rather than covering 50.
what_we_can_steal: THE ONE THING: move inference from the SENTENCE level to the CLAIM level. Extract claims into a store (claim, value, source_id, uncertainty signal), then run a separate CROSS-CHECK stage that computes RELATIONS between claims (agrees / contradicts / different-level-of-analysis / different-assumption). A synthesis sentence is then a rendering of a relation between two span-verified claims and cites BOTH -- so it is still fully grounded, but it is NOT extractable from any single span. This is exactly the move that unblocks our insight score without abandoning grounding. 40% of sourcery's cited sentences cite >=2 sources; ours are ~all single-source.

ADD THREE DEDICATED SYNTHESIS SECTIONS, each a direct rendering of a pipeline stage, not free prose: (1) 'Contradictions & Debates' -- ~7 named tensions, each stating thesis-with-cites vs antithesis-with-cites and then an explicit RESOLUTION sentence ('these are not contradictory -- they operate at different levels of analysis'); (2) 'Evidence Map' -- a table of Theme x Strongest / Moderate / Weak-or-Absent evidence, cites in every cell (this scores insight AND comprehensiveness for ~630 words); (3) 'Unknowns & Open Questions' -- ~10 numbered gaps, each grounded NEGATIVELY in what a specific source fails to cover ('Source 4 explicitly lacks data on wages'). Negative grounding is span-verifiable and reads as insight.

PLAN FOR CONTROVERSY UP FRONT. Sourcery's PLAN stage explicitly maps 'likely controversies' and its QUERY stage deliberately generates skeptical/critical-angle queries. If you never retrieve the counter-evidence, no downstream synthesis stage can find a tension. Our retrieval is probably consensus-biased.

RELAX 100% SPAN COVERAGE. Sourcery cites only 65.7% of sentences; the other third is interpretation and transition. Our all-or-nothing span rule is likely what forbids the connective tissue that reads as thought. Propose a two-tier rule: FACTUAL sentences must be span-verified; RELATIONAL sentences must cite every claim they relate (both sides); CONNECTIVE sentences may be uncited but may not introduce new facts.

DEPTH OVER BREADTH: 12 sources, ~31 citation instances each. Mining one source 30 times forces you to use its assumptions, methods, sample sizes, and limitations -- which is where the tension material lives. Consider capping sources and raising per-source extraction depth (methods, N, limitations, stated uncertainty) rather than adding sources.

MAKE CITATION A DETERMINISTIC POST-PASS. Body cites are HTML anchors rebuilt from the source list, so the writer LLM never has to hallucinate a reference. Decouple 'write with source ids' from 'render citations'.

A GENRE-INVARIANT FIXED TEMPLATE IS ENOUGH TO RANK 6. 86/100 identical section order, same scaffold for lit reviews and M&A. We do not need per-task outline planning to beat our current 43.82 -- but note the cost: sourcery's readability (49.68) is below the reference, so DO adapt heading language to the prompt language (it does not, and pays for it).
Section word budget as an explicit contract: Core Findings ~40% of the report, everything else 5-14%. Prevents the exec-summary-bloat / thin-body failure.
unknowns: The code is NOT public: github.com/freakynit/sourcery returns 404 as of 2026-07-13, despite the site saying 'source code publishing soon'. Everything about the internals below the 12 stage names is inferred from artifacts, not read.

WHICH LLM(s) it uses. Site says only 'LLM-agnostic / any OpenAI-compatible endpoint'. The benchmark submission's actual model is unknown. Given <$0.10/report it cannot be a frontier model at high token counts -- but that cost claim is vendor-stated and unverified.

Whether the fixed 11-section template is a hard-coded prompt scaffold or an LLM-chosen outline that happens to converge. 86/100 identical order strongly implies hard-coded, but we have not seen the prompt.
Whether 'Cross-check' contradiction detection is an LLM call over claim pairs, an embedding/clustering step, or rule-based on extracted numeric ranges. Unknown.
Whether each section is a separate LLM call. The per-section citation-format divergence and 'Source N' leakage make section-scoped generation very likely, but we cannot prove it from text alone.
Whether any revision/critic pass exists at all. We see no evidence of one over the prose; 'Verify' is described only as citation-to-source mapping, not factual critique.
Its FACT/citation-accuracy numbers are absent from our board ('-' columns), so we cannot confirm its grounding claims ('0 hallucinated claims') empirically.
